string.name: object_info_sun_description
string.translation_description: Short description of the Sun

resources.string: The Sun is the star at the center of our solar system. It provides the light and heat that makes life on Earth possible.

string.name: object_info_sun_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about the Sun

resources.string: The Sun contains 99.86% of all mass in our solar system. About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside it!

string.name: object_info_sun_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Sun shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 150 million km

string.name: object_info_sun_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Sun shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1.4 million km diameter

string.name: object_info_sun_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Sun in scientific notation — translate if needed for the locale.

string.name: object_info_moon_description
string.translation_description: Short description of the Moon

resources.string: The Moon is Earth\'s only natural satellite. It influences our tides and has been visited by 12 astronauts.

string.name: object_info_moon_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about the Moon

resources.string: The Moon is slowly drifting away from Earth at about 3.8 cm per year. It was much closer billions of years ago!

string.name: object_info_moon_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Moon shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

string.name: object_info_moon_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Moon shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 3,474 km diameter

string.name: object_info_moon_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Moon in scientific notation — translate if needed for the locale.

string.name: object_info_mercury_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Mercury

resources.string: Mercury is the smallest planet and closest to the Sun. A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days.

string.name: object_info_mercury_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Mercury

resources.string: Despite being closest to the Sun, Mercury is not the hottest planet. Venus is hotter due to its thick atmosphere!

string.name: object_info_mercury_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Mercury shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 58 million km (from Sun)

string.name: object_info_mercury_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Mercury shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 4,880 km diameter

string.name: object_info_mercury_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Mercury in scientific notation — translate if needed for the locale.

resources.string: 3.3 × 10²³ kg

string.name: object_info_venus_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Venus

resources.string: Venus is the second planet from the Sun and the hottest in our solar system. It\'s often called Earth\'s twin due to similar size.

string.name: object_info_venus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Venus

resources.string: A day on Venus is longer than its year! It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once, but only 225 days to orbit the Sun.

string.name: object_info_venus_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Venus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 108 million km (average)

string.name: object_info_venus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Venus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 12,104 km diameter

string.name: object_info_venus_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Venus in scientific notation — translate if needed for the locale.

string.name: object_info_mars_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Mars

resources.string: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun, known as the Red Planet due to iron oxide on its surface.

string.name: object_info_mars_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Mars

resources.string: Mars has the largest volcano in the solar system: Olympus Mons, which is about 2.5 times taller than Mount Everest!

string.name: object_info_mars_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Mars shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 228 million km (average)

string.name: object_info_mars_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Mars shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 6,779 km diameter

string.name: object_info_mars_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Mars in scientific notation — translate if needed for the locale.

string.name: object_info_jupiter_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Jupiter

resources.string: Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system. It\'s a gas giant with a famous Great Red Spot storm.

string.name: object_info_jupiter_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Jupiter

resources.string: Jupiter has at least 95 known moons! Its largest moon, Ganymede, is bigger than the planet Mercury.

string.name: object_info_jupiter_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Jupiter shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 778 million km (average)

string.name: object_info_jupiter_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Jupiter shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 139,820 km diameter

string.name: object_info_jupiter_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Jupiter in scientific notation — translate if needed for the locale.

string.name: object_info_saturn_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Saturn

resources.string: Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun, famous for its spectacular ring system made of ice and rock.

string.name: object_info_saturn_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Saturn

resources.string: Saturn is so light it would float in water! Its density is less than water, though there\'s no bathtub big enough to test this.

string.name: object_info_saturn_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Saturn shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1.4 billion km (average)

string.name: object_info_saturn_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Saturn shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 116,460 km diameter

string.name: object_info_saturn_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Saturn in scientific notation — translate if needed for the locale.

string.name: object_info_uranus_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Uranus

resources.string: Uranus is an ice giant that rotates on its side. It was the first planet discovered using a telescope.

string.name: object_info_uranus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Uranus

resources.string: Uranus rotates on its side at a 98-degree tilt, possibly due to a collision with an Earth-sized object long ago!

string.name: object_info_uranus_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Uranus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.9 billion km (average)

string.name: object_info_uranus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Uranus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 50,724 km diameter

string.name: object_info_uranus_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Uranus in scientific notation — translate if needed for the locale.

string.name: object_info_neptune_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Neptune

resources.string: Neptune is the most distant planet in our solar system. It has the strongest winds, reaching 2,100 km/h.

string.name: object_info_neptune_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Neptune

resources.string: Neptune was discovered through mathematics! Scientists predicted its location before anyone saw it through a telescope.

string.name: object_info_neptune_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Neptune shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 4.5 billion km (average)

string.name: object_info_neptune_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Neptune shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 49,244 km diameter

string.name: object_info_neptune_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Neptune in scientific notation — translate if needed for the locale.

string.name: object_info_pluto_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Pluto

resources.string: Pluto is a dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt. It was considered the ninth planet until 2006.

string.name: object_info_pluto_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Pluto

resources.string: Pluto has a heart-shaped glacier made of nitrogen ice, visible in images from NASA\'s New Horizons mission!

string.name: object_info_pluto_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Pluto shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 5.9 billion km (average)

string.name: object_info_pluto_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Pluto shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2,377 km diameter

string.name: object_info_pluto_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Pluto in scientific notation — translate if needed for the locale.

resources.string: 1.3 × 10²² kg

string.name: object_info_sirius_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Sirius

resources.string: Sirius is the brightest star in the night sky, located in the constellation Canis Major (the Greater Dog).

string.name: object_info_sirius_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Sirius

resources.string: Sirius is actually a binary star system! Sirius B, its companion, is a white dwarf about the size of Earth but nearly as massive as the Sun.

string.name: object_info_sirius_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Sirius shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 8.6 light-years

string.name: object_info_sirius_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Sirius shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1.7 solar radii

string.name: object_info_sirius_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Sirius in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.1 solar masses

string.name: object_info_vega_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Vega

resources.string: Vega is one of the brightest stars visible from Earth, located in the constellation Lyra (the Harp).

string.name: object_info_vega_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Vega

resources.string: Vega was the North Star about 14,000 years ago and will be again in about 12,000 years due to Earth\'s wobble!

string.name: object_info_vega_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Vega shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 25 light-years

string.name: object_info_vega_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Vega shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.4 solar radii

string.name: object_info_vega_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Vega in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.1 solar masses

string.name: object_info_betelgeuse_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Betelgeuse

resources.string: Betelgeuse is a red supergiant star marking the shoulder of Orion. It\'s one of the largest stars visible to the naked eye.

string.name: object_info_betelgeuse_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Betelgeuse

resources.string: Betelgeuse is so large that if placed at our Sun\'s position, it would engulf Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, and possibly Jupiter!

string.name: object_info_betelgeuse_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Betelgeuse shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~700 light-years

string.name: object_info_betelgeuse_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Betelgeuse shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~900 solar radii

string.name: object_info_betelgeuse_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Betelgeuse in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~12 solar masses

string.name: object_info_rigel_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Rigel

resources.string: Rigel is a blue supergiant star marking the foot of Orion. Despite being farther than Betelgeuse, it appears similarly bright.

string.name: object_info_rigel_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Rigel

resources.string: Rigel shines with the light of about 120,000 Suns! It\'s one of the most luminous stars in our region of the Milky Way.

string.name: object_info_rigel_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Rigel shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~860 light-years

string.name: object_info_rigel_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Rigel shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~79 solar radii

string.name: object_info_rigel_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Rigel in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~23 solar masses

string.name: object_info_procyon_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Procyon

resources.string: Procyon is a bright star in the constellation Canis Minor (the Lesser Dog). It\'s one of our closer stellar neighbors.

string.name: object_info_procyon_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Procyon

resources.string: Procyon means "before the dog" in Greek because it rises just before Sirius, the Dog Star, in the night sky!

string.name: object_info_procyon_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Procyon shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 11.4 light-years

string.name: object_info_procyon_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Procyon shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.0 solar radii

string.name: object_info_procyon_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Procyon in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1.5 solar masses

string.name: object_info_polaris_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Polaris

resources.string: Polaris, the North Star, appears nearly motionless in the sky while other stars rotate around it throughout the night.

string.name: object_info_polaris_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Polaris

resources.string: Polaris is actually a triple star system! The main star is a supergiant about 2,500 times brighter than our Sun.

string.name: object_info_polaris_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Polaris shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~430 light-years

string.name: object_info_polaris_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Polaris shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~44 solar radii

string.name: object_info_polaris_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Polaris in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~5.4 solar masses

string.name: object_info_deneb_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Deneb

resources.string: Deneb marks the tail of Cygnus (the Swan). It\'s part of the Summer Triangle asterism along with Vega and Altair.

string.name: object_info_deneb_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Deneb

resources.string: Deneb is one of the most distant stars visible to the naked eye, located about 2,600 light-years away!

string.name: object_info_deneb_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Deneb shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~2,600 light-years

string.name: object_info_deneb_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Deneb shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~200 solar radii

string.name: object_info_deneb_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Deneb in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~19 solar masses

string.name: object_info_arcturus_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Arcturus

resources.string: Arcturus is the brightest star in the northern hemisphere, an orange giant in the constellation Boötes (the Herdsman).

string.name: object_info_arcturus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Arcturus

resources.string: Arcturus is traveling through space at a tremendous speed and will pass closest to our solar system in about 4,000 years!

string.name: object_info_arcturus_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Arcturus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 37 light-years

string.name: object_info_arcturus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Arcturus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 26 solar radii

string.name: object_info_arcturus_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Arcturus in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1.1 solar masses

string.name: object_info_canopus_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Canopus

resources.string: Canopus is the second-brightest star in the night sky. It\'s visible mainly from the Southern Hemisphere.

string.name: object_info_canopus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Canopus

resources.string: Canopus is used by spacecraft for navigation because it\'s very bright and far from the ecliptic plane!

string.name: object_info_canopus_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Canopus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 310 light-years

string.name: object_info_canopus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Canopus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 72 solar radii

string.name: object_info_canopus_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Canopus in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 8.0 solar masses

string.name: object_info_aldebaran_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Aldebaran

resources.string: Aldebaran is an orange giant star that represents the eye of Taurus (the Bull). Its name means "the follower" in Arabic.

string.name: object_info_aldebaran_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Aldebaran

resources.string: Aldebaran appears to be part of the Hyades star cluster but is actually only half the distance to us - a cosmic optical illusion!

string.name: object_info_aldebaran_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Aldebaran shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 65 light-years

string.name: object_info_aldebaran_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Aldebaran shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 44 solar radii

string.name: object_info_aldebaran_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Aldebaran in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1.2 solar masses

string.name: object_info_antares_description
string.translation_description: Short description of Antares

resources.string: Antares is a red supergiant star, the heart of Scorpius (the Scorpion). Its name means "rival of Mars" due to its red color.

string.name: object_info_antares_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Antares

resources.string: Antares is so large that if placed in our solar system, its surface would extend past the orbit of Mars!

string.name: object_info_antares_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Antares shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~550 light-years

string.name: object_info_antares_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Antares shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~680 solar radii

string.name: object_info_antares_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Antares in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~12 solar masses

string.name: object_info_capella_description
string.translation_description: Description of Capella shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Capella is the brightest star in Auriga and the sixth-brightest in the night sky. It\'s actually a system of four stars.

string.name: object_info_capella_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Capella shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Capella means "little goat" in Latin. Ancient cultures saw it as a goat carried on the shoulder of Auriga, the Charioteer!

string.name: object_info_capella_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Capella shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 43 light-years

string.name: object_info_capella_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Capella shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 12 solar radii

string.name: object_info_capella_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Capella in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.6 solar masses

string.name: object_info_altair_description
string.translation_description: Description of Altair shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Altair is the brightest star in Aquila (the Eagle) and one of the closest stars visible to the naked eye.

string.name: object_info_altair_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Altair shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Altair rotates so fast (once every 9 hours) that it\'s noticeably flattened at its poles - it\'s 14% wider than it is tall!

string.name: object_info_altair_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Altair shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 17 light-years

string.name: object_info_altair_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Altair shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1.8 solar radii

string.name: object_info_altair_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Altair in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1.8 solar masses

string.name: object_info_spica_description
string.translation_description: Description of Spica shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Spica is the brightest star in Virgo and one of the 20 brightest stars in the night sky. It\'s a binary star system.

string.name: object_info_spica_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Spica shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Spica is so close to the ecliptic that it can be occulted by the Moon. The name means "ear of wheat" in Latin!

string.name: object_info_spica_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Spica shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 250 light-years

string.name: object_info_spica_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Spica shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 7.5 solar radii

string.name: object_info_spica_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Spica in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 11 solar masses

string.name: object_info_achernar_description
string.translation_description: Description of Achernar shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Achernar is the brightest star in Eridanus (the River) and the ninth-brightest star in the night sky.

string.name: object_info_achernar_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Achernar shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Achernar is the flattest star known! It spins so fast that its equatorial diameter is 56% larger than its polar diameter.

string.name: object_info_achernar_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Achernar shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 139 light-years

string.name: object_info_achernar_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Achernar shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 7.3 solar radii

string.name: object_info_achernar_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Achernar in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 6.7 solar masses

string.name: object_info_hadar_description
string.translation_description: Description of Hadar shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Hadar (Beta Centauri) is the second-brightest star in Centaurus and the 11th brightest in the night sky.

string.name: object_info_hadar_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Hadar shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Hadar and Alpha Centauri are known as the "Southern Pointers" - they point to the Southern Cross constellation!

string.name: object_info_hadar_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Hadar shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 390 light-years

string.name: object_info_hadar_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Hadar shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 9 solar radii

string.name: object_info_hadar_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Hadar in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 12.8 solar masses

string.name: object_info_regulus_description
string.translation_description: Description of Regulus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Regulus is the brightest star in Leo (the Lion) and represents the lion\'s heart. It\'s a multiple star system.

string.name: object_info_regulus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Regulus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Regulus spins so fast it\'s nearly tearing itself apart! It completes a rotation in just 16 hours.

string.name: object_info_regulus_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Regulus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 79 light-years

string.name: object_info_regulus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Regulus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 4.4 solar radii

string.name: object_info_regulus_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Regulus in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 3.8 solar masses

string.name: object_info_acrux_description
string.translation_description: Description of Acrux shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Acrux (Alpha Crucis) is the brightest star in the Southern Cross and the 13th brightest star in the sky.

string.name: object_info_acrux_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Acrux shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Acrux appears on the flags of Australia, New Zealand, Brazil, Papua New Guinea, and Samoa!

string.name: object_info_acrux_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Acrux shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 320 light-years

string.name: object_info_acrux_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Acrux shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 7.8 solar radii

string.name: object_info_acrux_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Acrux in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 18 solar masses

string.name: object_info_castor_description
string.translation_description: Description of Castor shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Castor is one of the twin stars in Gemini. Though it appears as one star, it\'s actually a system of six stars!

string.name: object_info_castor_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Castor shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Castor is a sextuple star system - three pairs of binary stars all orbiting each other. It\'s one of the most complex star systems near Earth!

string.name: object_info_castor_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Castor shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 51 light-years

string.name: object_info_castor_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Castor shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.4 solar radii

string.name: object_info_castor_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of Castor in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.8 solar masses

string.name: object_info_pollux_description
string.translation_description: Description of Pollux shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Pollux is the brighter of the twin stars in Gemini. Unlike Castor, it\'s a single orange giant star.

string.name: object_info_pollux_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Pollux shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Pollux has a confirmed exoplanet! Pollux b is a gas giant at least twice as massive as Jupiter.

resources.string: Fomalhaut is the brightest star in Piscis Austrinus and one of the brightest stars in the autumn sky.

string.name: object_info_fomalhaut_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Fomalhaut shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Fomalhaut has a spectacular debris disk that was one of the first to be directly imaged around another star!

string.name: object_info_adhara_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Adhara shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: About 4.7 million years ago, Adhara was the brightest star in the night sky, even brighter than Sirius is today!

resources.string: Shaula is the second-brightest star in Scorpius and marks the scorpion\'s stinger in the tail.

string.name: object_info_shaula_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Shaula shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Shaula means "the raised tail" in Arabic. It\'s actually a triple star system with the stars very close together!

resources.string: Alnilam is the middle star of Orion\'s Belt and one of the most luminous stars known in our galaxy.

string.name: object_info_alnilam_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Alnilam shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Alnilam is losing mass at a rate of 20 million times faster than our Sun through its powerful stellar wind!

string.name: object_info_alphard_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Alphard shown in the info card. Translate this text.

string.name: object_info_mirach_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Mirach shown in the info card. Translate this text.

string.name: object_info_algol_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Algol shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Every 2.87 days, Algol dims noticeably as its dimmer companion passes in front - ancient astronomers found this spooky!

string.translation_description: Description of M1 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Crab Nebula is a supernova remnant in Taurus, the result of a stellar explosion recorded by Chinese astronomers in 1054 AD.

string.name: object_info_m1_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M1 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Crab Nebula contains a pulsar spinning 30 times per second, the collapsed core of the exploded star!

string.translation_description: Description of M13 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M13 is a spectacular globular cluster in Hercules containing about 300,000 stars, one of the brightest in the northern sky.

string.name: object_info_m13_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M13 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: In 1974, the Arecibo message was beamed toward M13 as an attempt to contact extraterrestrial intelligence!

string.translation_description: Description of M31 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Andromeda Galaxy is the nearest large spiral galaxy to the Milky Way and one of the most distant objects visible to the naked eye.

string.name: object_info_m31_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M31 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Andromeda Galaxy is approaching us at 110 km/s and will collide with the Milky Way in about 4.5 billion years!

string.translation_description: Description of M42 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

string.name: object_info_m42_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M42 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Orion Nebula is a stellar nursery where new stars are being born right now. Over 700 stars are forming inside!

string.name: object_info_m45_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M45 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Pleiades are among the nearest star clusters to Earth and have been recognized by virtually every ancient civilization!

string.name: object_info_m51_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M51 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Whirlpool was the first galaxy to be recognized as having spiral structure, discovered by Lord Rosse in 1845!

string.translation_description: Description of M57 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Ring Nebula is a planetary nebula in Lyra, a glowing shell of gas expelled by a dying Sun-like star.

string.name: object_info_m57_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M57 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Ring Nebula is expanding at about 20 km/s and will eventually disperse into space over the next 10,000 years!

string.translation_description: Description of M81 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M81 is a grand-design spiral galaxy in Ursa Major, one of the brightest galaxies visible from Earth.

string.name: object_info_m81_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M81 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M81 is gravitationally interacting with neighboring galaxy M82, triggering intense star formation in M82!

string.name: object_info_m82_description
string.translation_description: Description of M82 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Cigar Galaxy is a starburst galaxy in Ursa Major, known for its edge-on orientation and intense star formation.

string.name: object_info_m82_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M82 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M82 is forming stars 10 times faster than our Milky Way due to gravitational interaction with neighboring M81!

string.name: object_info_m101_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M101 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Pinwheel Galaxy contains over a trillion stars and has asymmetric spiral arms due to past galaxy interactions!

string.name: object_info_m104_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M104 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Sombrero Galaxy has a supermassive black hole at its center estimated to be 1 billion solar masses!

string.translation_description: Description of M8 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Lagoon Nebula is a giant interstellar cloud in Sagittarius, one of the finest and brightest nebulae in the summer sky.

string.name: object_info_m8_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M8 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Lagoon Nebula contains Herschel 36, one of the most massive and luminous stars known!

string.translation_description: Description of M17 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

string.name: object_info_m17_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M17 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Omega Nebula contains an open cluster of about 800 young stars hidden within its gas and dust!

string.translation_description: Description of M20 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

string.name: object_info_m20_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M20 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Trifid shows three distinct types of nebulae in one object: red emission, blue reflection, and dark absorption!

string.translation_description: Description of M27 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

string.name: object_info_m27_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M27 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Dumbbell Nebula was discovered by Charles Messier in 1764, making it the first planetary nebula ever discovered!

string.translation_description: Fun fact about M33 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Triangulum Galaxy may be gravitationally bound to the Andromeda Galaxy as a distant satellite!

string.name: object_info_m44_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M44 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Ancient astronomers used the visibility of the Beehive Cluster to predict weather - if invisible, rain was coming!

string.translation_description: Fun fact about M78 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

string.translation_description: Fun fact about M83 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M83 has produced six observed supernovae, more than any other galaxy - a record for supernova production!

string.translation_description: Fun fact about M5 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

string.translation_description: Fun fact about M12 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

string.translation_description: Fun fact about M64 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

string.name: object_info_m106_description
string.translation_description: Description of M106 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M106 is a spiral galaxy in Canes Venatici with anomalous spiral arms caused by jets from its central black hole.

string.name: object_info_m106_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M106 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M106 has water masers in its nucleus, allowing astronomers to precisely measure its distance!

string.name: object_info_m106_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to M106 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 23.5 million light-years

string.name: object_info_m106_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of M106 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 135,000 light-years across

string.name: object_info_m109_description
string.translation_description: Description of M109 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M109 is a barred spiral galaxy in Ursa Major, the brightest member of a galaxy group bearing its name.

string.name: object_info_m109_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M109 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M109 is located very close to the star Phecda in the Big Dipper, making it easy to locate!

string.name: object_info_m109_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to M109 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 83 million light-years

string.name: object_info_m109_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of M109 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 180,000 light-years across

string.name: object_info_m110_description
string.translation_description: Description of M110 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M110 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy and satellite of the Andromeda Galaxy, the last object added to the Messier catalog.

string.name: object_info_m110_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M110 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M110 was actually observed by Messier in 1773 but not added to his catalog until 1966!

string.name: object_info_m110_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to M110 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.7 million light-years

string.name: object_info_m110_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of M110 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 17,000 light-years across

string.name: object_info_m32_description
string.translation_description: Description of M32 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M32 is a compact dwarf elliptical galaxy and close satellite of the Andromeda Galaxy.

string.name: object_info_m32_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M32 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M32 was the first elliptical galaxy ever discovered and may have once been a larger spiral galaxy stripped by Andromeda!

string.name: object_info_m32_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to M32 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.5 million light-years

string.name: object_info_m32_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of M32 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 6,500 light-years across

string.name: object_info_m49_description
string.translation_description: Description of M49 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M49 is a giant elliptical galaxy in Virgo, the brightest member of the Virgo Cluster.

string.name: object_info_m49_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M49 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M49 was the first member of the Virgo Cluster to be discovered and contains about 6,000 globular clusters!

string.name: object_info_m49_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to M49 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 55 million light-years

string.name: object_info_m49_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of M49 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 160,000 light-years across

string.name: object_info_m87_description
string.translation_description: Description of M87 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M87 is a supergiant elliptical galaxy in Virgo, one of the most massive galaxies in the local universe.

string.name: object_info_m87_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about M87 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: M87\'s central black hole was the first ever imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope in 2019 - it has 6.5 billion solar masses!

string.name: object_info_m87_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to M87 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 53 million light-years

string.name: object_info_m87_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of M87 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 240,000 light-years across

string.name: object_info_m87_mass
string.translation_description: Mass of M87 in scientific notation . Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 2.4 trillion solar masses

string.name: object_info_t_crb_description
string.translation_description: Description of T Crb shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: T Coronae Borealis (T CrB) is a recurrent nova in Corona Borealis — a binary system of a red giant and a white dwarf. The white dwarf periodically accumulates enough hydrogen from its companion to trigger a thermonuclear explosion.

string.name: object_info_t_crb_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about T Crb shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: T CrB last erupted in 1946, briefly reaching magnitude 2.0 and becoming visible to the naked eye. Astronomers predict that another eruption is imminent — keep watching!

string.name: object_info_t_crb_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to T Crb shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 3,000 light-years

string.name: object_info_t_crb_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of T Crb shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 0.2 AU orbital separation

string.name: object_info_tarantula_nebula_description
string.translation_description: Description of the Tarantula Nebula shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Tarantula Nebula is the largest and most luminous star-forming region in the Local Group, a vast emission nebula embedded in the Large Magellanic Cloud spanning some 600 light-years. Despite lying 163,000 light-years away, it is bright enough to be visible to the naked eye.

string.name: object_info_tarantula_nebula_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about the Tarantula Nebula shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: If the Tarantula Nebula were as close as the Orion Nebula, it would be bright enough to cast shadows at night and would span half the visible sky!

string.name: object_info_tarantula_nebula_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to the Tarantula Nebula shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 163,000 light-years

string.name: object_info_tarantula_nebula_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of the Tarantula Nebula shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 600 light-years across

string.name: object_info_lmc_description
string.translation_description: Description of the Large Magellanic Cloud shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Large Magellanic Cloud is an irregular satellite galaxy of the Milky Way, visible to the naked eye as a detached, glowing patch of sky. It is one of our closest galactic neighbors and one of the most active star-forming galaxies in the Local Group.

string.name: object_info_lmc_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about the Large Magellanic Cloud shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: In 1987, a star in the LMC exploded as Supernova 1987A — the first naked-eye supernova seen since 1604. Neutrinos from the blast reached Earth three hours before the light, confirming our models of stellar core collapse.

string.name: object_info_lmc_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to the Large Magellanic Cloud shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 160,000 light-years

string.name: object_info_lmc_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of the Large Magellanic Cloud shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 14,000 light-years across

string.name: object_info_smc_description
string.translation_description: Description of the Small Magellanic Cloud shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Small Magellanic Cloud is a dwarf irregular satellite galaxy of the Milky Way, paired with its larger sibling the LMC. Together they are slowly being torn apart by the Milky Way\'s gravity, trailing a vast stream of gas across the sky.

string.name: object_info_smc_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about the Small Magellanic Cloud shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: By studying Cepheid variable stars in the SMC in 1908–12, Henrietta Swan Leavitt discovered the period-luminosity law — the breakthrough that first let astronomers measure distances across the universe!

string.name: object_info_smc_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to the Small Magellanic Cloud shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 200,000 light-years

string.name: object_info_smc_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of the Small Magellanic Cloud shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 7,000 light-years across

string.name: object_info_willman_1_description
string.translation_description: Description of Willman 1 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Willman 1 is an ultra-faint dwarf satellite galaxy of the Milky Way, discovered in 2004 by Beth Willman in data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey. It is one of the least luminous galaxies known and one of the most extreme laboratories for dark matter in the universe.

string.name: object_info_willman_1_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Willman 1 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: For every unit of ordinary matter in Willman 1, there are roughly 660 units of dark matter — making it among the most dark-matter-dominated objects ever discovered. Its stars also show signs of tidal disruption as the Milky Way slowly pulls it apart.

string.name: object_info_willman_1_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Willman 1 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~130,000 light-years

string.name: object_info_willman_1_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Willman 1 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~130 light-years across

string.name: object_info_sgr_a_star_description
string.translation_description: Description of Sagittarius A* shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Sagittarius A* (Sgr A*) is the supermassive black hole at the centre of the Milky Way, lying 26,000 light-years away in the direction of Sagittarius. With a mass of about 4 million solar masses compressed into a region smaller than our solar system, it is so dense that not even light can escape its event horizon.

string.name: object_info_sgr_a_star_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Sagittarius A* shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: In 2022 the Event Horizon Telescope collaboration released the first direct image of Sgr A*, revealing a glowing ring of superheated gas surrounding its shadow — produced by combining data from radio observatories spread across the entire planet.

string.name: object_info_sgr_a_star_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Sgr A* shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 26,000 light-years

string.name: object_info_sgr_a_star_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Sgr A* shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: Event horizon diameter: ~24 million km

string.name: object_info_cygnus_x_1_description
string.translation_description: Description of Cygnus X-1 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Cygnus X-1 is a stellar-mass black hole about 7,200 light-years away in Cygnus, and one of the strongest X-ray sources in the sky. It forms a binary system with the blue supergiant star HDE 226868, orbiting each other every 5.6 days. The black hole strips gas from its companion, forming a superhot accretion disc that blazes in X-rays.

string.name: object_info_cygnus_x_1_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Cygnus X-1 shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Cygnus X-1 was the first object widely accepted as a black hole. In 1975, Stephen Hawking made a famous scientific wager with Kip Thorne that it was NOT a black hole, a bet he later conceded.

string.name: object_info_cygnus_x_1_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Cygnus X-1 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 7,200 light-years

string.name: object_info_cygnus_x_1_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Cygnus X-1 shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: Black hole mass: ~21 solar masses

string.name: object_info_eta_carinae_nebula_description
string.translation_description: Description of Eta Carinae Nebula shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Eta Carinae Nebula (NGC 3372) is one of the largest and brightest nebulae in the sky, spanning over 300 light-years. At its heart sits Eta Carinae, one of the most massive and luminous stars known, weighing over 100 solar masses.

string.name: object_info_eta_carinae_nebula_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Eta Carinae Nebula shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: In 1843 Eta Carinae erupted and briefly became the second-brightest star in the sky, yet somehow it survived — it may be on the verge of going supernova!

string.name: object_info_eta_carinae_nebula_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to Eta Carinae Nebula shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 7,500 light-years

string.name: object_info_eta_carinae_nebula_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Eta Carinae Nebula shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 300 light-years across

string.name: object_info_north_america_nebula_description
string.translation_description: Description of the North America Nebula shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The North America Nebula (NGC 7000) is a large emission nebula in Cygnus whose shape remarkably resembles the continent of North America, complete with a Gulf of Mexico region. Spanning about 120 arcminutes across — roughly four times the diameter of the full Moon — it lies around 2,600 light-years away and is over 100 light-years wide.

string.name: object_info_north_america_nebula_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about the North America Nebula shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Despite its visual magnitude of 4, the nebula is too large and diffuse to see with the naked eye under most skies — yet it photographs beautifully, revealing the Cygnus Wall, a dense arc of intense star formation along its southern edge.

string.name: object_info_north_america_nebula_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to the North America Nebula shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~2,590 light-years

string.name: object_info_north_america_nebula_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of the North America Nebula shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~140 light-years across

string.name: object_info_jewel_box_description
string.translation_description: Description of the Jewel Box cluster shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Jewel Box (NGC 4755) is a young, brilliant open cluster in the constellation Crux, easily visible to the naked eye as a hazy fourth-magnitude patch just southeast of Mimosa. When viewed through a telescope, its stars blaze in vivid contrasting colours — deep blue supergiants alongside a striking red supergiant — earning it the name John Herschel gave it: a superb piece of fancy jewellery. At only 14–16 million years old, it is one of the youngest and most colourful clusters in the sky.

string.name: object_info_jewel_box_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about the Jewel Box shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The cluster\'s standout red star, κ Crucis, is a red supergiant so luminous it outshines the Sun by more than 100,000 times — a vivid reminder that star clusters are not all the same colour.

string.name: object_info_jewel_box_distance
string.translation_description: Distance to the Jewel Box shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~6,500 light-years

string.name: object_info_jewel_box_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of the Jewel Box shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: ~19 light-years across

string.name: object_info_orion_description
string.translation_description: Description of Orion shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Orion the Hunter is one of the most recognizable constellations, visible from both hemispheres with its distinctive Belt of three stars.

string.name: object_info_orion_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Orion shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Orion\'s Belt points to Sirius (the brightest star) in one direction and Aldebaran (in Taurus) in the other!

string.name: object_info_orion_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Orion shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 594 square degrees

string.name: object_info_ursa_major_description
string.translation_description: Description of Ursa Major shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Ursa Major (the Great Bear) contains the Big Dipper asterism, one of the most recognized star patterns in the northern sky.

string.name: object_info_ursa_major_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Ursa Major shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Five of the Big Dipper stars are physically related, moving through space together as the Ursa Major Moving Group!

string.name: object_info_ursa_major_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Ursa Major shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1,280 square degrees

string.name: object_info_ursa_minor_description
string.translation_description: Description of Ursa Minor shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Ursa Minor (the Little Bear) contains the Little Dipper and Polaris, the current North Star marking Earth\'s celestial north pole.

string.name: object_info_ursa_minor_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Ursa Minor shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Due to precession, Polaris won\'t always be the North Star - in 14,000 years, Vega will take its place!

string.name: object_info_ursa_minor_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Ursa Minor shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 256 square degrees

string.name: object_info_cassiopeia_description
string.translation_description: Description of Cassiopeia shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Cassiopeia is a distinctive W-shaped constellation in the northern sky, named after a vain queen in Greek mythology.

string.name: object_info_cassiopeia_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Cassiopeia shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Tycho Brahe\'s supernova of 1572 appeared in Cassiopeia, helping to prove that the heavens could change!

string.name: object_info_cassiopeia_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Cassiopeia shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 598 square degrees

string.name: object_info_scorpius_description
string.translation_description: Description of Scorpius shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Scorpius is a prominent zodiac constellation with a distinctive curved tail, dominated by the red supergiant Antares.

string.name: object_info_scorpius_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Scorpius shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: In mythology, Scorpius was placed in the sky opposite Orion so the hunter would never be stung again!

string.name: object_info_scorpius_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Scorpius shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 497 square degrees

string.name: object_info_leo_description
string.translation_description: Description of Leo shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Leo the Lion is a zodiac constellation featuring Regulus at the heart of the lion and the distinctive Sickle asterism.

string.name: object_info_leo_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Leo shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Leonid meteor shower in November appears to radiate from Leo and can produce spectacular storms!

string.name: object_info_leo_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Leo shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 947 square degrees

string.name: object_info_gemini_description
string.translation_description: Description of Gemini shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Gemini the Twins features the bright stars Castor and Pollux, representing the twin brothers from Greek mythology.

string.name: object_info_gemini_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Gemini shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Despite being called twins, Castor is actually six stars and Pollux is an orange giant - they look nothing alike!

string.name: object_info_gemini_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Gemini shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 514 square degrees

string.name: object_info_taurus_description
string.translation_description: Description of Taurus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Taurus the Bull is a zodiac constellation containing the Pleiades and Hyades star clusters, and the bright star Aldebaran.

string.name: object_info_taurus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Taurus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Crab Nebula in Taurus is the remnant of a supernova witnessed and recorded by Chinese astronomers in 1054 AD!

string.name: object_info_taurus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Taurus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 797 square degrees

string.name: object_info_virgo_description
string.translation_description: Description of Virgo shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Virgo is the second largest constellation and a zodiac sign, containing the Virgo Cluster of over 2,000 galaxies.

string.name: object_info_virgo_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Virgo shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Virgo Cluster is the heart of the Virgo Supercluster, which contains our own Milky Way galaxy!

string.name: object_info_virgo_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Virgo shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1,294 square degrees

string.name: object_info_aquarius_description
string.translation_description: Description of Aquarius shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Aquarius the Water Bearer is a zodiac constellation in a region of sky often called the Sea, surrounded by water-related constellations.

string.name: object_info_aquarius_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Aquarius shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Aquarius contains the Helix Nebula, one of the closest planetary nebulae to Earth at only 700 light-years away!

string.name: object_info_aquarius_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Aquarius shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 980 square degrees

string.name: object_info_pisces_description
string.translation_description: Description of Pisces shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Pisces the Fishes is a large zodiac constellation representing two fish tied together, marking the vernal equinox point.

string.name: object_info_pisces_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Pisces shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The vernal equinox (first day of spring) currently lies in Pisces and has been slowly moving through it for 2,000 years!

string.name: object_info_pisces_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Pisces shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 889 square degrees

string.name: object_info_aries_description
string.translation_description: Description of Aries shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Aries the Ram is a zodiac constellation that was once home to the vernal equinox, giving us the phrase "First Point of Aries."

string.name: object_info_aries_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Aries shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Though the vernal equinox moved to Pisces about 2,000 years ago, we still call it the First Point of Aries!

string.name: object_info_aries_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Aries shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 441 square degrees

string.name: object_info_cancer_description
string.translation_description: Description of Cancer shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Cancer the Crab is the faintest zodiac constellation but contains the famous Beehive Cluster (M44) visible to the naked eye.

string.name: object_info_cancer_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Cancer shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Tropic of Cancer was named when the Sun was in Cancer at the summer solstice, though it\'s now in Taurus!

string.name: object_info_cancer_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Cancer shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 506 square degrees

string.name: object_info_capricornus_description
string.translation_description: Description of Capricornus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Capricornus the Sea-Goat is an ancient zodiac constellation depicting a mythical creature that\'s half goat, half fish.

string.name: object_info_capricornus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Capricornus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Capricornus is one of the oldest recognized constellations, appearing in Babylonian star catalogs over 3,000 years ago!

string.name: object_info_capricornus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Capricornus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 414 square degrees

string.name: object_info_sagittarius_description
string.translation_description: Description of Sagittarius shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Sagittarius the Archer points toward the center of our Milky Way galaxy, making it rich in nebulae and star clusters.

string.name: object_info_sagittarius_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Sagittarius shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: When you look at Sagittarius, you\'re looking toward Sagittarius A*, the supermassive black hole at our galaxy\'s center!

string.name: object_info_sagittarius_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Sagittarius shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 867 square degrees

string.name: object_info_libra_description
string.translation_description: Description of Libra shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Libra the Scales is the only zodiac constellation representing an inanimate object, once considered part of Scorpius.

string.name: object_info_libra_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Libra shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Libra\'s main stars were once known as the Claws of the Scorpion before becoming the Scales of Justice!

string.name: object_info_libra_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Libra shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 538 square degrees

string.name: object_info_cygnus_description
string.translation_description: Description of Cygnus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Cygnus the Swan flies along the Milky Way, containing the Northern Cross asterism and the bright star Deneb.

string.name: object_info_cygnus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Cygnus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Cygnus X-1 in this constellation was the first widely accepted black hole, identified in the early 1970s!

string.name: object_info_cygnus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Cygnus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 804 square degrees

string.name: object_info_lyra_description
string.translation_description: Description of Lyra shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Lyra the Lyre is a small but prominent constellation containing Vega, the fifth brightest star in the night sky.

string.name: object_info_lyra_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Lyra shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Vega was the North Star around 12,000 BC and will be again in about 12,000 years due to Earth\'s precession!

string.name: object_info_lyra_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Lyra shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 286 square degrees

string.name: object_info_aquila_description
string.translation_description: Description of Aquila shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Aquila the Eagle is a constellation on the celestial equator, containing the bright star Altair at its center.

string.name: object_info_aquila_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Aquila shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Altair in Aquila is one of the fastest rotating stars known, spinning so fast it bulges at its equator!

string.name: object_info_aquila_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Aquila shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 652 square degrees

string.name: object_info_pegasus_description
string.translation_description: Description of Pegasus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Pegasus the Winged Horse is a large constellation featuring the Great Square of Pegasus, a prominent autumn asterism.

string.name: object_info_pegasus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Pegasus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: 51 Pegasi b, discovered in 1995 in this constellation, was the first exoplanet found orbiting a Sun-like star!

string.name: object_info_pegasus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Pegasus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1,121 square degrees

string.name: object_info_andromeda_description
string.translation_description: Description of Andromeda shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Andromeda the Princess contains the Andromeda Galaxy (M31), the nearest large spiral galaxy to our Milky Way.

string.name: object_info_andromeda_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Andromeda shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: The Andromeda Galaxy is approaching us at 110 km/s and will merge with the Milky Way in about 4.5 billion years!

string.name: object_info_andromeda_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Andromeda shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 722 square degrees

string.name: object_info_perseus_description
string.translation_description: Description of Perseus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Perseus the Hero is home to the famous variable star Algol and the Double Cluster, a stunning pair of open clusters.

string.name: object_info_perseus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Perseus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Algol (the Demon Star) was considered evil in ancient times because it mysteriously dims every 2.87 days!

string.name: object_info_perseus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Perseus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 615 square degrees

string.name: object_info_centaurus_description
string.translation_description: Description of Centaurus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Centaurus the Centaur is a large southern constellation containing Alpha Centauri, the nearest star system to our Sun.

string.name: object_info_centaurus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Centaurus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Proxima Centauri in Centaurus is only 4.24 light-years away, making it the closest star to our solar system!

string.name: object_info_centaurus_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Centaurus shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 1,060 square degrees

string.name: object_info_crux_description
string.translation_description: Description of Crux shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Crux (the Southern Cross) is the smallest constellation but one of the most distinctive, used for navigation in the Southern Hemisphere.

string.name: object_info_crux_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Crux shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Despite being the smallest constellation, Crux has more bright stars per unit area than any other constellation!

string.name: object_info_crux_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Crux shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 68 square degrees

string.name: object_info_canis_major_description
string.translation_description: Description of Canis Major shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Canis Major (the Great Dog) follows Orion across the sky and contains Sirius, the brightest star visible from Earth.

string.name: object_info_canis_major_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Canis Major shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Ancient Egyptians based their calendar on the heliacal rising of Sirius, which coincided with the Nile\'s annual flood!

string.name: object_info_canis_major_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Canis Major shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 380 square degrees

string.name: object_info_canis_minor_description
string.translation_description: Description of Canis Minor shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Canis Minor (the Lesser Dog) is a small constellation notable for Procyon, the eighth brightest star in the sky.

string.name: object_info_canis_minor_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Canis Minor shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Procyon means "before the dog" in Greek because it rises just before Sirius (the Dog Star) each night!

string.name: object_info_canis_minor_size
string.translation_description: Physical size of Canis Minor shown in the info card. Translate units if appropriate for your locale.

resources.string: 183 square degrees

string.name: object_info_cepheus_description
string.translation_description: Description of Cepheus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Cepheus the King is a northern constellation whose stars include Delta Cephei, the prototype for Cepheid variable stars.

string.name: object_info_cepheus_funfact
string.translation_description: Fun fact about Cepheus shown in the info card. Translate this text.

resources.string: Cepheid variables discovered by Henrietta Leavitt revolutionized astronomy by allowing us to measure cosmic distances!

string.name: object_info_cepheus_size
